title: Benchmarking Quantum Annealing for a Greenhouse-Inspired Control QUBO
abstract: We benchmark current annealing-based optimization workflows on a greenhouse-inspired quadratic unconstrained binary optimization problem for binary heater scheduling, where the horizon H denotes the number of hourly control decisions. For the main one-day instance (H=24), all solver outputs are decoded back into heater schedules and evaluated in the original greenhouse simulator using the same physical objective and feasibility criterion. Classical simulated annealing and path-integral simulated quantum annealing produce feasible near-optimal solutions in all repetitions, with best objectives close to the exact optimum. In contrast, the tested D-Wave Leap Hybrid BQM workflow is less reliable and does not outperform the classical baselines under 15--60~s requested time limits. Direct D-Wave QPU execution on reduced instances remains feasible in all runs and recovers the exact optimum for H=10 and H=12, but the exact-hit rate drops from 5/10 to 2/10 and then to 0/10 at H=14, with substantially higher variance than the classical baselines. The results do not indicate quantum advantage, but provide a reproducible, physically decoded benchmark that exposes the current strengths and limitations of classical, hybrid, and direct quantum annealing workflows on structured control QUBOs.
summary: This paper benchmarks classical, hybrid, and direct quantum annealing solvers on a practical optimization task involving greenhouse climate control. It finds that while quantum-inspired and classical methods are highly reliable, current D-Wave hybrid and direct QPU workflows do not yet demonstrate quantum advantage for this structured control problem.
main_problem: Benchmarking the performance, reliability, and feasibility of various quantum and classical optimization workflows on a structured QUBO problem derived from greenhouse climate control.
main_result: Classical simulated annealing and path-integral simulated quantum annealing outperform the D-Wave Leap Hybrid BQM and direct QPU execution for the tested H=24 instance, with the QPU exact-hit rate dropping significantly as problem size increases.
method: Comparison of exact enumeration, classical Simulated Annealing (SA), Path-Integral Simulated Quantum Annealing (PISA), D-Wave Leap Hybrid BQM, and direct D-Wave QPU execution.
model_or_system: A greenhouse thermal model featuring air and canopy temperature dynamics, driven by solar radiation, outdoor temperature, and electricity tariffs, formulated as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem for heater scheduling.
key_observables: Decoded objective value (growth, energy, and switching costs), solution feasibility (temperature bound violations), and exact-hit rate for QPU execution.
important_parameters: Time horizon H (up to 24 hours), temperature bounds (16-26 degrees C), and electricity tariff structures.
main_assumptions: The model assumes instantaneous heater effects (no lag) and uses a simplified quadratic proxy for plant growth rather than a fully calibrated agronomic model.
figures_summary: The figures include an overview of the greenhouse simulator (thermal trajectories, solar radiation, and tariffs), body-temperature trajectories for feasible solutions, and cumulative growth proxy plots.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a greenhouse-inspired control problem, formulates it as a QUBO, describes the various classical and quantum solvers used, presents a benchmark comparison of solution quality and feasibility, and discusses the limitations of current quantum annealing workflows.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Best feasible air-temperature trajectories for the H = 24 benchmark. Only the best feasible decoded solution of each solver workflow is shown to keep the comparison readable. The admissible interval is defined by Tmin = 16◦C and Tmax = 26◦C.
